Daily US-Israel-Iran War Brief:
25th Jun 2026:

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief

● U.S. officials reported that Iran fired on a cargo ship attempting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, shortly after the vessel reported being hit by a projectile.

● An Israeli drone strike on a vehicle in southern Lebanon killed three people and wounded one. The Israeli military stated it targeted operatives who posed a threat during the incident.

● Senior Israeli and Lebanese officials denied that there has been any Israeli withdrawal from occupied southern Lebanon. This contradicts claims of a troop pullback made by a U.S. official.

● The U.S. Secretary of State announced that Washington seeks a lasting peace with Iran, provided it does not compromise the security of allied nations in the region.

● Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned that vessels crossing the Strait of Hormuz without authorization will face consequences. They also rejected a newly announced route for the waterway.

END OF THE BRIEF

temporary routes (north and south of the main shipping lane) for safe departure of vessels.

tweets.title: BREAKING: J&K Police Files FIR Against Rashtriya Rifles Personnel in Kishtwar Incident Jammu & Kashmir Police have registered an FIR against the Commanding Officer of 17 Rashtriya Rifles, a Major, a Naib Subedar, and 30–40 Army personnel over an alleged incident at Atholi Police Station, Kishtwar, on June 24. The FIR includes charges under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), including attempt to murder, and the Prevention of Damage to Public Property Act. Police allege Army personnel entered the police station, assaulted officers, and damaged government property. The Indian Army has acknowledged the incident, said it is examining the matter through established institutional mechanisms, and stated it will fully cooperate with the investigation. The allegations are under investigation, and no judicial findings have been made.
tweets.content:
BREAKING: J&K Police Files FIR Against Rashtriya Rifles Personnel in Kishtwar Incident

Jammu & Kashmir Police have registered an FIR against the Commanding Officer of 17 Rashtriya Rifles, a Major, a Naib Subedar, and 30–40 Army personnel over an alleged incident at Atholi Police Station, Kishtwar, on June 24.

The FIR includes charges under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), including attempt to murder, and the Prevention of Damage to Public Property Act. Police allege Army personnel entered the police station, assaulted officers, and damaged government property.

The Indian Army has acknowledged the incident, said it is examining the matter through established institutional mechanisms, and stated it will fully cooperate with the investigation. The allegations are under investigation, and no judicial findings have been made.

A major security scare has emerged from Chirawa, Rajasthan, after a man allegedly posing as an Indian Army Lieutenant Colonel and ECHS Vigilance Officer gained access to multiple private hospitals and examined sensitive records related to ex-servicemen and their families. The suspect reportedly convinced hospital administrations with his knowledge of military healthcare procedures and inspected OPD, admission, and treatment records before raising suspicion.

The alarm was triggered when hospital authorities noticed inconsistencies in his conduct and questioned his credentials. After ECHS officials intervened and attempted verification, the suspect failed to answer technical military questions and fled the area before he could be detained. A report has now been forwarded to Army Headquarters, while security agencies investigate whether this was an attempted fraud, data collection operation, or part of a larger network exploiting military identity.

The incident has exposed serious concerns regarding the misuse of military uniforms, unauthorized access to sensitive records, and verification protocols at institutions dealing with defence personnel and veterans.

tweets.title: DRDO’s Archer-NG UAV to Gain Strike Capability India's indigenous Archer-NG UAV is being developed beyond its surveillance role into a multi-role combat platform capable of engaging tanks, armoured vehicles, and even aerial targets. Key reported upgrades include: 1. Integration of anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) and Smart Anti-Airfield Weapons (SAAW) 2. Planned integration of the Astra Mk-1 beyond-visual-range air-to-air missile (BVRAAM) for engaging hostile aircraft 3. AESA radar to enhance target detection, tracking, and engagement 4. Retention of its primary intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance (ISTAR) role while adding precision-strike capability. If these capabilities are successfully integrated and validated, the Archer-NG will become one of India's first indigenous UAVs capable of performing both surveillance and offensive combat missions, significantly expanding the operational flexibility of the Indian Armed Forces.
tweets.content:
DRDO’s Archer-NG UAV to Gain Strike Capability

India's indigenous Archer-NG UAV is being developed beyond its surveillance role into a multi-role combat platform capable of engaging tanks, armoured vehicles, and even aerial targets.

Key reported upgrades include:

1. Integration of anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) and Smart Anti-Airfield Weapons (SAAW)

2. Planned integration of the Astra Mk-1 beyond-visual-range air-to-air missile (BVRAAM) for engaging hostile aircraft

3. AESA radar to enhance target detection, tracking, and engagement

4. Retention of its primary intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance (ISTAR) role while adding precision-strike capability.

If these capabilities are successfully integrated and validated, the Archer-NG will become one of India's first indigenous UAVs capable of performing both surveillance and offensive combat missions, significantly expanding the operational flexibility of the Indian Armed Forces.

This shift comes after commercial talks with US-based General Electric (GE) hit a major roadblock over surging cost demands (nearly 300% higher than early estimates) and restrictive technology transfer terms.
tweets.content:
India is exploring joint engine development with France's Safran and the UK's Rolls-Royce for the AMCA fighter project. This shift comes after commercial talks with US-based General Electric (GE) hit a major roadblock over surging cost demands (nearly 300% higher than early estimates) and restrictive technology transfer terms.

Netram Defence Review (@NetramDefence)

GE demands 300% cost increase for AMCA engines.

India’s negotiations with GE Aerospace over the F414 fighter jet engine have reportedly stalled after the projected cost surged from ₹70–80 crore per engine to over ₹200 crore during commercial discussions.

The F414 is slated to power the HAL Tejas Mk2 and serve as the interim engine for the Advanced Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA Mk-1).

- Engine cost up nearly 300%
- Proposed Indian assembly line could require over $800 million (~₹6,000 crore) in investment
- Negotiations continue over technology transfer, localisation, production, and support packages
- India is also evaluating alternative partnerships with Safran and Rolls-Royce
